titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Grimston family correspondence

editionstmt.p: M. Rinn revised the finding aid in 2025.

publicationstmt.publisher: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday April 16, 2026 at 18:00

did.unittitle: Grimston family letters

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

did.abstract: This collection is compromised of correspondence to Effie (Florence Maria) Grimston Byrom between the years 1872-1889. The bulk of the letters are written by her older siser Rose Armatrude frances Grimston Hobart, with additional writing from other family members. These personal accounts shed light into the private lives of women in the late 1800s.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection is compromised of correspondence to Effie (Florence Maria) Grimston Byrom. The bulk of the letters are written by her older siser Rose Armatrude Frances Grimston Hobart, along with correspondence from other members of Effie's and Rose's family and friends.

Letters sent between the two sisters are personal and lively. They discuss their family homes, ancedotes about family members and other events. Correspondence from other family members include surprise at learning of the birth of her first child from a newspaper and child rearing advice. The letter from a friend named Eda not only discusses children, but reveal's Eda's travels in France and her use of cocaine for hay fever.

Also present in the collection is a small note about travels, a clipping covering Rose's wedding, and a printed handkerchief showing a cartoon called "Granny's Ride." The item is referred to in a letter and shows an old woman being dragged in a cart by three children and a man.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Fund

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Grimston Family Correspondence, Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Fund

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The collection is the physical property of the Yale Center for British Art. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the Archives Department.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Letters are arranged chronologically with additional material placed at the end.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Rose Armatrude Frances Grimston Hobart (1859-1927 November 20) was the first child of Colonel Marmaduke Jerald Grimston (1826-1879) and Florence Victoria Saunderson (1838-1914). She was born in Yorkshire, England.

As evidenced through their correspondence, Rose was immensely close with her younger sister Florence (affectionately called Effie.) The two kept in constant contact with each other, especially once Effie move into her husband Edward's home in Devon.

In 1889, Rose married Colonel George Bertie Benjamin Hobart (1838-), an event which warranted coverage in The Court Journal . They had two children together, Armatrude Bertie Sophia Effie Hobart (1890-1982) and Anna Hobart (1890-). The family appears to have lived in Scotland, London, and Rose's native Yorkshire throughout the years.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Florence Maria Grimston Byrom (1857 June 18 - 1920 Nov 6) was the second child of Colonel Marmaduke Jerald Grimston (1826-1879) and Florence Victoria Saunderson (1838-1914). She was born in Yorkshire, but the family moved to London in the 1879s.
Her sister and family referred to her as Effie, and she was especially close with her sister rose. Their letters to each other reveal their sisterly affection across the miles.

On 1882 October 9, Effie married her husband Edward Fox Byrom, D.L. (deputy lieutenant), who would later go on to be appointed Sheriff of Devon for the year 1888. The couple spent most of their lives in Devon and had two children together: Rosie Effie Jeraldine Byrom (1884-1968) and Edward Lutterll Grimston Byrom (1885-1944). They primarily resided in Byrom's family home, Culver House, located in the parish of Holcombe Burnell, Exeter.|
Effie died on 1920 November 6. She is buried in East Riding of Yorkshire Unitary Authority with her husband Edward.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

scopecontent.p: May be written by one of Effie's three uncles, Walter, William, or Alexander.
